terms.definition: Command-line interface — a program invoked from a shell prompt, taking arguments via argv and returning results via stdout/stderr and an exit code. Also called a console application.

A wrapper script generated by pip from a `[project.scripts]` entry point; lives on PATH (e.g. `<venv>/bin/mycli`) and invokes a Python callable. The mechanism that turns a Python function into an installed command.
A library that turns `sys.argv` into typed Python values and generates `--help` automatically. Stdlib: `argparse`. Popular third-party: `click`, `typer`, `docopt`.
A second-level command that follows the tool name, e.g. `git commit`, `pip install`. Implemented with `argparse.add_subparsers()` or framework equivalents.
The integer returned by a process when it terminates (also called return code or status code). `0` means success; non-zero means failure. Read by shells (`$?`), CI runners, and pipelines.
The three default I/O channels every process has: stdin (input), stdout (results), stderr (messages/errors). Shell pipes route stdout; stderr stays attached to the terminal.
Python invocation mode that runs a module as a script (the module runner): `python -m pkg.mod` executes `pkg/mod/__main__.py` or the module's `if __name__ == '__main__'` block.
The `if __name__ == '__main__':` block (also called the main guard) that lets a file act as both an importable module and a runnable script. Code inside runs only on direct execution, not on import.

The `#!/usr/bin/env python` line (also called hashbang) at the top of an executable file that tells Unix which interpreter to use. Not needed for pip-installed console scripts — pip generates the wrapper.
